title: From the standpoint of
meaning: From the standpoint, perspective, or domain of something — used to frame a statement within a particular field or context.
explanation: Noun + 上 (じょう) indicates that what follows is true or relevant within the scope of a specific domain, such as law, form, or appearance (法律上, 形式上, 見かけ上). It is a formal, written-register expression and is very common in academic, legal, and business Japanese. It differs from ～にとって in that 上 restricts the domain rather than expressing the viewpoint of a person.

examples.japanese: 法律上は問題ないが、倫理的には疑問が残る。
examples.reading: ほうりつじょうはもんだいないが、りんりてきにはぎもんがのこる。
examples.english: There is no problem from a legal standpoint, but ethical questions remain.

examples.japanese: 書類上は在籍しているが、実際には働いていない。
examples.reading: しょるいじょうはざいせきしているが、じっさいにははたらいていない。
examples.english: On paper he is still enrolled, but in reality he is not working.

examples.japanese: 健康上の理由で、出張を断らざるを得なかった。
examples.reading: けんこうじょうのりゆうで、しゅっちょうをことわらざるをえなかった。
examples.english: For health reasons, I had no choice but to decline the business trip.

title: Since / Now that (obligation/consequence)
meaning: Since, now that, or as long as — used to indicate that given a certain situation or decision, a natural consequence or obligation follows.
explanation: Verb (plain form) + 以上（は） establishes a premise from which the speaker draws a logical or moral conclusion — often an obligation, expectation, or resolution. The nuance is stronger than から or ので; it implies 'given that this is the case, there is no choice but to…' or 'this being so, one must…'. It is a formal expression found mainly in written Japanese and formal speech.

examples.japanese: 引き受けた以上は、最後まで責任を持ってやり遂げなければならない。
examples.reading: ひきうけたいじょうは、さいごまでせきにんをもってやりとげなければならない。
examples.english: Now that I've taken it on, I must see it through to the end with full responsibility.

examples.japanese: チームのリーダーである以上、メンバーのことを最優先に考えるべきだ。
examples.reading: チームのリーダーであるいじょう、メンバーのことをさいゆうせんにかんがえるべきだ。
examples.english: Since you are the team leader, you should put the members' needs first.

examples.japanese: 参加すると決めた以上、途中で諦めるわけにはいかない。
examples.reading: さんかするときめたいじょう、とちゅうであきらめるわけにはいかない。
examples.english: Since I decided to participate, I can't give up halfway.

title: For the first time in (a period)
meaning: Indicates that an action is happening for the first time after a specified period of absence or non-occurrence.
explanation: Attaches directly to a time expression (e.g., 3年ぶりに, 久しぶりに) to mark that the action hasn't occurred for that length of time and is now happening again. It implies a significant gap and often carries emotional weight — reunion, nostalgia, or relief. Commonly used in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: 5年ぶりに故郷に帰って、懐かしい気持ちになった。
examples.reading: 5ねんぶりにこきょうにかえって、なつかしいきもちになった。
examples.english: Returning to my hometown for the first time in five years, I felt a wave of nostalgia.

examples.japanese: 久しぶりに会った友人は、すっかり大人になっていた。
examples.reading: ひさしぶりにあったゆうじんは、すっかりおとなになっていた。
examples.english: The friend I met for the first time in ages had grown up completely.

examples.japanese: 1か月ぶりに晴れたので、洗濯物をたくさん干した。
examples.reading: 1かげつぶりにはれたので、せんたくものをたくさんほした。
examples.english: Since it was the first sunny day in a month, I hung out a lot of laundry.

title: As one hopes / As desired
meaning: Expresses that something goes (or doesn't go) the way one had hoped, intended, or imagined.
explanation: 思うように is most commonly used in negative sentences — 思うようにいかない / できない — to mean things aren't going as hoped. In positive contexts it describes a situation where something proceeds exactly as wished. The pattern is neutral in register and very natural in both spoken and written Japanese when discussing outcomes versus expectations.

examples.japanese: 練習を重ねているのに、思うように上達しなくて悔しい。
examples.reading: れんしゅうをかさねているのに、おもうようにじょうたつしなくてくやしい。
examples.english: Even though I keep practicing, it's frustrating that I'm not improving as much as I hoped.

examples.japanese: 体調が悪くて、仕事が思うように進まなかった。
examples.reading: たいちょうがわるくて、しごとがおもうようにすすまなかった。
examples.english: I wasn't feeling well, so the work didn't progress the way I had hoped.

examples.japanese: 今日は思うように走れて、自己ベストを更新できた。
examples.reading: きょうはおもうようにはしれて、じこベストをこうしんできた。
examples.english: Today I was able to run just as I had hoped and set a new personal best.

title: だけに
meaning: As might be expected, Because, All the more because, It's only natural
explanation: Grammar explanation for だけに

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Potential/possibility suffix
meaning: Expresses that something is possible or can occur.
explanation: Attach 得る (うる/える) to the conjunctive (masu-stem) form of a verb to indicate that something is possible or conceivable. It is more formal and literary than できる and often appears in written, academic, or professional contexts. The reading うる is more classical; える is more common in modern usage.

examples.japanese: そのような事故は十分に起こり得る。
examples.reading: そのようなじこはじゅうぶんにおこりうる。
examples.english: Such an accident can very well happen.

examples.japanese: 彼女はあらゆる可能性を考え得る人物だ。
examples.reading: かのじょはあらゆるかのうせいをかんがえうるじんぶつだ。
examples.english: She is a person who can consider every possibility.

examples.japanese: この条件のもとでは、様々な結果が生じ得ます。
examples.reading: このじょうけんのもとでは、さまざまなけっかがしょうじえます。
examples.english: Under these conditions, various outcomes can occur.

title: On the contrary / Conversely
meaning: Introduces a result or perspective that is the opposite of what was just stated or expected.
explanation: 逆に is an adverb used to signal a reversal or contrast with the preceding statement, introducing an outcome, effect, or viewpoint that runs in the opposite direction. It can express genuine contradiction ('on the contrary, it got worse'), ironic reversal ('I tried to help but ended up causing trouble instead'), or a shift in perspective ('conversely, you could look at it this way'). It is common in both casual speech and formal writing.

examples.japanese: 節約しようとしたのに、逆に余計なお金を使ってしまった。
examples.reading: せつやくしようとしたのに、ぎゃくによけいなおかねをつかってしまった。
examples.english: I was trying to save money, but I ended up spending even more instead.

examples.japanese: 批判されるかと思ったが、逆に大きな支持を得た。
examples.reading: ひはんされるかとおもったが、ぎゃくにおおきなしじをえた。
examples.english: I thought I would be criticized, but on the contrary, I received strong support.

examples.japanese: そのアドバイスは逆効果で、問題が逆に悪化した。
examples.reading: そのアドバイスはぎゃくこうかで、もんだいがぎゃくにあっかした。
examples.english: That advice backfired, and the problem actually got worse as a result.

title: From the perspective of / Judging by
meaning: Indicates that a judgment or assessment is being made from a specific person's viewpoint or based on a particular appearance or evidence.
explanation: から見ると combines から (from) with 見る (to look/see) and と to mean 'looking from' or 'judging from.' It is used to indicate whose perspective is being taken or what evidence is being used to reach a conclusion. It is neutral in register and appears in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: 外から見ると、その建物はとても古そうだが、中はきれいだった。
examples.reading: そとからみると、そのたてものはとてもふるそうだが、なかはきれいだった。
examples.english: From the outside, the building looked very old, but the inside was clean.

examples.japanese: 専門家から見ると、この絵は本物ではないと判断できる。
examples.reading: せんもんかからみると、このえはほんものではないとはんだんできる。
examples.english: From an expert's point of view, it can be determined that this painting is not authentic.

examples.japanese: 子供から見ると、大人の世界はとても複雑に映るだろう。
examples.reading: こどもからみると、おとなのせかいはとてもふくざつにうつるだろう。
examples.english: From a child's perspective, the adult world must appear very complicated.

title: でしかない
meaning: Merely, Nothing but, No more than
explanation: Grammar explanation for でしかない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Scheduled to, arranged to
meaning: To be expected to, to be scheduled to, to be arranged that
explanation: The nominalizer こと combined with になっている expresses what is scheduled, arranged, or expected to happen according to a plan or rule. It describes established facts about what is supposed to occur. Common in formal contexts, instructions, and announcements.

examples.japanese: 明日の会議は午前十時に始まることになっている。
examples.reading: あしたのかいぎはごぜんじゅうじにはじまることになっている。
examples.english: The meeting tomorrow is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m.

examples.japanese: この計画では来月から新しいプロジェクトが始まることになっている。
examples.reading: このけいかくではらいげつからあたらしいぷろじぇくとがはじまることになっている。
examples.english: According to this plan, a new project is scheduled to start next month.

examples.japanese: 会社のルールでは、休暇は一か月前に申請することになっている。
examples.reading: かいしゃのるーるでは、きゅうかはいっかげつまえにしんせいすることになっている。
examples.english: According to company rules, leave is supposed to be applied for one month in advance.

title: も～ば～も
meaning: …And…, Both ... and ..., ... And ... as well
explanation: Grammar explanation for も～ば～も

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

pattern: に向かって・に向けて
title: Towards / Directed at
meaning: Expresses physical direction towards a place or person, or effort and preparation aimed at a goal.
explanation: に向かって indicates movement or orientation physically towards something or someone, while に向けて more strongly implies purposeful preparation or action directed at a future goal or target audience. Both are used at neutral register in everyday and formal speech. に向けて is particularly common in project, event, or campaign contexts.

examples.japanese: 彼女は駅に向かって走り出した。
examples.reading: かのじょはえきにむかってはしりだした。
examples.english: She started running towards the station.

examples.japanese: 来年の試験に向けて、毎日勉強している。
examples.reading: らいねんのしけんにむけて、まいにちべんきょうしている。
examples.english: I am studying every day in preparation for next year's exam.

examples.japanese: スピーチで彼は観客に向かって力強いメッセージを伝えた。
examples.reading: スピーチでかれはかんきゃくにむかってちからづよいメッセージをつたえた。
examples.english: In his speech, he delivered a powerful message directly to the audience.

title: From the point of view of
meaning: Expresses something from the perspective or standpoint of a particular person, indicating how things appear or feel to them.
explanation: にしたら follows a noun referring to a person or group and expresses that what follows is how things seem, feel, or are judged from that person's perspective. It is similar to にとって (for / to someone) but specifically highlights a subjective viewpoint, often involving feelings or judgments that might differ from others'. Related forms include にしてみれば and にすれば. Register is neutral to slightly informal.

examples.japanese: 子供にしたら、その規則は意味がわからないだろう。
examples.reading: こどもにしたら、そのきそくはいみがわからないだろう。
examples.english: From a child's point of view, those rules probably make no sense.

examples.japanese: 彼女にしたら、突然のキャンセルはとても失礼に感じたはずだ。
examples.reading: かのじょにしたら、とつぜんのキャンセルはとてもしつれいにかんじたはずだ。
examples.english: From her perspective, the sudden cancellation must have felt very rude.

examples.japanese: 初心者にしたら、この操作は難しく感じるかもしれない。
examples.reading: しょしんしゃにしたら、このそうさはむずかしくかんじるかもしれない。
examples.english: From the perspective of a beginner, this operation might feel difficult.

title: Regardless of whether A or B
meaning: Used to state that the same conclusion or situation applies no matter which of two options is chosen or assumed.
explanation: にしても～にしても connects two contrasting options (often antonyms or opposing choices) and asserts that the outcome or judgment is the same in either case. It is neutral in register and works in both spoken and written Japanese. Use it when you want to emphasize that no option changes the result.

examples.japanese: 行くにしても行かないにしても、返事は早めにしてください。
examples.reading: いくにしてもいかないにしても、へんじははやめにしてください。
examples.english: Whether you go or not, please reply as soon as possible.

examples.japanese: 成功するにしても失敗するにしても、全力で挑戦することが大切だ。
examples.reading: せいこうするにしてもしっぱいするにしても、ぜんりょくでちょうせんすることがたいせつだ。
examples.english: Whether you succeed or fail, what matters is giving it your all.

examples.japanese: 買うにしても借りるにしても、まず予算を決めたほうがいい。
examples.reading: かうにしてもかりるにしても、まずよさんをきめたほうがいい。
examples.english: Whether you buy it or rent it, you should first decide on a budget.

title: にすぎない
meaning: No more than, Just, Merely
explanation: Grammar explanation for にすぎない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: しかも
meaning: Moreover, Furthermore
explanation: Grammar explanation for しかも

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Speaking of which, Come to think of it
meaning: A discourse marker used to introduce a related thought or topic that was triggered by something just said or noticed.
explanation: そういえば is a conversational connector that signals the speaker has just recalled something relevant to the current topic. It is used to smoothly shift to a related subject without it feeling abrupt. It is casual to neutral in register and is extremely common in everyday spoken Japanese.

examples.japanese: そういえば、昨日田中さんに会いましたよ。
examples.reading: そういえば、きのうたなかさんにあいましたよ。
examples.english: Come to think of it, I ran into Tanaka-san yesterday.

examples.japanese: そういえば、明日会議があるって言ってたね。
examples.reading: そういえば、あしたかいぎがあるっていってたね。
examples.english: Speaking of which, you mentioned there's a meeting tomorrow, right?

examples.japanese: そういえば、あの映画もう公開されたんだっけ？
examples.reading: そういえば、あのえいがもうこうかいされたんだっけ？
examples.english: Come to think of it, has that movie been released yet?

title: Acting as if / with the intention of
meaning: Expresses acting with a particular intention, pretending something is the case, or proceeding under a mistaken or assumed belief.
explanation: つもりで follows the plain form of a verb or a noun with の to indicate that the speaker acts as though a condition were true, or undertakes an action while treating a substitute as the real thing. It can also describe situations where someone mistakenly believed something. The nuance shifts based on context: volitional (with the intention of doing) or pretend/substitution (acting as if it were).

examples.japanese: 本番のつもりで、もう一度練習しましょう。
examples.reading: ほんばんのつもりで、もういちどれんしゅうしましょう。
examples.english: Let's practice once more, treating it as if it were the real thing.

examples.japanese: 旅行に行ったつもりで、その分のお金を貯金した。
examples.reading: りょこうにいったつもりで、そのぶんのおかねをちょきんした。
examples.english: Pretending I had gone on a trip, I saved the money I would have spent.

examples.japanese: 彼は冗談のつもりで言ったが、相手は本気にしてしまった。
examples.reading: かれはじょうだんのつもりでいったが、あいてはほんきにしてしまった。
examples.english: He said it intending it as a joke, but the other person took it seriously.

title: Get by without doing something
meaning: Expresses that one manages to avoid doing something that would otherwise be necessary or unpleasant.
explanation: ずに済む combines the negative ず-form (literary negative) with 済む (to be enough, to get by). It expresses relief or convenience that an action turned out to be unnecessary. The plain-form equivalent ないで済む is also common and slightly more colloquial. Both forms appear in everyday speech and writing when describing avoided burdens or lucky outcomes.

examples.japanese: 友達が手伝ってくれたので、残業せずに済んだ。
examples.reading: ともだちがてつだってくれたので、ざんぎょうせずにすんだ。
examples.english: Since my friend helped me, I managed to get by without working overtime.

examples.japanese: オンラインで申し込めば、窓口に行かずに済む。
examples.reading: オンラインでもうしこめば、まどぐちにいかずにすむ。
examples.english: If you apply online, you can get away with not going to the counter.

examples.japanese: 傘を持っていたので、濡れずに済んだ。
examples.reading: かさをもっていたので、ぬれずにすんだ。
examples.english: Since I had an umbrella, I managed not to get wet.

title: だけあって
meaning: Is only natural, …Being the case, Because, As might be expected
explanation: Grammar explanation for だけあって

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Impossibility suffix
meaning: Expresses that something cannot happen or is impossible.
explanation: Formed by attaching 得ない (えない/うない) to the masu-stem of a verb, this pattern states that something is impossible or inconceivable. It is the negative counterpart of 得る and similarly carries a formal, written register. It is often used to dismiss something as out of the question.

examples.japanese: 彼がそんな嘘をつくとは考え得ない。
examples.reading: かれがそんなうそをつくとはかんがええない。
examples.english: I cannot imagine him telling such a lie.

examples.japanese: この計画が失敗するとは、あり得ない話だ。
examples.reading: このけいかくがしっぱいするとは、ありえないはなしだ。
examples.english: The idea that this plan would fail is simply impossible.

examples.japanese: 科学的な根拠なしに、その結論は受け入れ得ない。
examples.reading: かがくてきなこんきょなしに、そのけつろんはうけいれえない。
examples.english: Without scientific basis, that conclusion cannot be accepted.

title: In addition to / On top of that
meaning: In addition to, on top of, or as well as — used to stack a second quality or fact onto the first, often intensifying the overall impression.
explanation: Noun + である上に / い-adjective + 上に / な-adjective + な上に / verb (plain form) + 上に is used to add a cumulative point, where both elements typically share the same positive or negative direction. It is more formal than それに or しかも and is common in written Japanese and formal speech. The key nuance is accumulation: the second item amplifies or compounds the first.

examples.japanese: 彼女は頭がいい上に、努力家でもある。
examples.reading: かのじょはあたまがいいうえに、どりょくかでもある。
examples.english: She is intelligent, and on top of that, she is also hardworking.

examples.japanese: その店は値段が高い上に、サービスも悪いので人気がない。
examples.reading: そのみせはねだんがたかいうえに、サービスもわるいのでにんきがない。
examples.english: That restaurant is expensive, and on top of that, the service is poor, so it's not popular.

examples.japanese: 道に迷った上に、財布まで落としてしまった。
examples.reading: みちにまよったうえに、さいふまでおとしてしまった。
examples.english: I got lost, and to make matters worse, I even dropped my wallet.

title: More than / Even more than
meaning: More than, no less than, or even more than — used to express that something exceeds a stated standard or expectation.
explanation: Noun / verb phrase + 以上に indicates that the degree of something surpasses the named benchmark, often with a sense of surprise or emphasis. It commonly follows a word like 予想、思った、想像 to mean 'more than expected/imagined.' It is used in both spoken and written Japanese and is relatively neutral in register, though more common in formal contexts.

examples.japanese: 試験は思った以上に難しくて、時間が足りなかった。
examples.reading: しけんはおもったいじょうにむずかしくて、じかんがたりなかった。
examples.english: The exam was more difficult than I expected, and I ran out of time.

examples.japanese: その映画は想像以上に感動的で、思わず涙が出た。
examples.reading: そのえいがはそうぞういじょうにかんどうてきで、おもわずなみだがでた。
examples.english: The film was even more moving than I had imagined, and tears came unbidden.

examples.japanese: この仕事は経験以上に、熱意が大切だと感じる。
examples.reading: このしごとはけいけんいじょうに、ねついがたいせつだとかんじる。
examples.english: In this job, I feel that passion matters even more than experience.

title: Hearsay or vague report: I heard that… or something
meaning: Used in casual speech to convey something heard secondhand or to soften a statement by making it sound approximate or non-committal.
explanation: When とか appears at or near the end of a clause, it signals that the speaker is reporting something they heard but is distancing themselves from full certainty, similar to 'something like' or 'I hear that.' It is distinctly casual and often used in conversation among peers. Overuse can make speech sound evasive or uncommitted.

examples.japanese: 彼、来月転勤になるとか言ってたよ。
examples.reading: かれ、らいげつてんきんになるとかいってたよ。
examples.english: I heard he said something about getting transferred next month.

examples.japanese: あの店、閉まるとか聞いたけど本当？
examples.reading: あのみせ、しまるとかきいたけどほんとう？
examples.english: I heard something about that shop closing — is it true?

examples.japanese: 今日、部長が早退するとか。
examples.reading: きょう、ぶちょうがそうたいするとか。
examples.english: Apparently the manager is leaving early today or something.

pattern: からすると・からすれば
title: Judging from, From the perspective of
meaning: Indicates a standpoint or basis from which a judgment or inference is made.
explanation: からすると and からすれば are largely interchangeable; both attach to a noun representing a viewpoint, standard, or piece of evidence and introduce a conclusion drawn from it. からすれば can carry a slightly stronger sense of the speaker adopting that perspective personally. Both are neutral in register and common in analytical speech and writing.

examples.japanese: 専門家からすると、この計画にはまだ問題点が多い。
examples.reading: せんもんかからすると、このけいかくにはまだもんだいてんがおおい。
examples.english: From an expert's perspective, this plan still has many problems.

examples.japanese: 彼の態度からすれば、もう諦めているのかもしれない。
examples.reading: かれのたいどからすれば、もうあきらめているのかもしれない。
examples.english: Judging from his attitude, he may have already given up.

examples.japanese: 常識からすると、そんな行動は理解しにくい。
examples.reading: じょうしきからすると、そんなこうどうはりかいしにくい。
examples.english: Judging by common sense, that kind of behavior is hard to understand.

bunproSlug: からすると-からすれば

title: Even if we assume / Even as
meaning: Grants a hypothetical or conceded premise and asserts that the outcome remains unaffected or is still valid.
explanation: としても introduces a concession — even if we take a stated situation as true, the conclusion still holds. It differs from ても in that としても often involves a more deliberate or reasoned hypothesis rather than a simple condition. It is neutral in register and common in both spoken discussion and formal writing.

examples.japanese: それが事実だとしても、すぐに対応するのは難しい。
examples.reading: それがじじつだとしても、すぐにたいおうするのはむずかしい。
examples.english: Even if that is a fact, it would be difficult to respond immediately.

examples.japanese: 急いで出発したとしても、会議には間に合わないだろう。
examples.reading: いそいでしゅっぱつしたとしても、かいぎにはまにあわないだろう。
examples.english: Even if we left in a hurry, we probably wouldn't make it to the meeting in time.

examples.japanese: 彼が謝ったとしても、失った信頼はすぐには戻らない。
examples.reading: かれがあやまったとしても、うしなったしんらいはすぐにはもどらない。
examples.english: Even if he apologized, the trust that was lost won't come back right away.

title: ことだから
meaning: It is exactly because
explanation: Grammar explanation for ことだから

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Negative volitional / Probably not
meaning: Expresses the speaker's firm intention not to do something, or a conjecture that something probably will not happen.
explanation: まい attaches directly to the dictionary form of most verbs (する → するまい / しまい, くる → くるまい / くまい) and functions as the negative counterpart of the volitional form. It carries two core uses: (1) strong personal resolve 'I will not…' and (2) negative conjecture 'probably won't…'. It is formal and literary in tone; it is rarely heard in casual conversation but appears in writing, formal speeches, and set expressions like 二度とするまい.

examples.japanese: あんな失敗は二度と繰り返すまいと心に誓った。
examples.reading: あんなしっぱいはにどとくりかえすまいとこころにちかった。
examples.english: I swore to myself that I would never repeat such a mistake again.

examples.japanese: これほど難しい試験は、彼でも合格できるまい。
examples.reading: これほどむずかしいしけんは、かれでもごうかくできるまい。
examples.english: An exam this difficult — even he probably won't be able to pass it.

examples.japanese: もう彼女には頼むまいと決めた。
examples.reading: もうかのじょにはたのむまいときめた。
examples.english: I decided I would not ask her for help anymore.

title: Without worrying about / Regardless of
meaning: Expresses doing something without regard for or in spite of a concern, inconvenience, or social expectation.
explanation: も構わず attaches to nouns or verb phrases to indicate that the subject proceeds with an action without minding or worrying about something that would normally be a consideration. It often implies boldness, carelessness, or urgency. It is neutral in register and used in both spoken and written Japanese, sometimes with a slightly critical nuance.

examples.japanese: 彼は周りの目も構わず、大声で電話をしていた。
examples.reading: かれはまわりのめもかまわず、おおごえででんわをしていた。
examples.english: He was talking on the phone loudly, without any regard for the people around him.

examples.japanese: 雨も構わず、子どもたちは外で遊び続けた。
examples.reading: あめもかまわず、こどもたちはそとであそびつづけた。
examples.english: The children kept playing outside without caring about the rain.

examples.japanese: 時間も構わず、彼女は深夜に友人に電話をかけた。
examples.reading: じかんもかまわず、かのじょはしんやにゆうじんにでんわをかけた。
examples.english: Without worrying about the time, she called her friend in the middle of the night.

title: That means / In other words
meaning: Used to draw a logical conclusion or restate the implication of what was just said.
explanation: This conjunction connects a statement to its natural consequence or rephrasing, signaling that the speaker is interpreting or clarifying the meaning of something. It can appear in both spoken and written Japanese and is neutral in register. It is often used in conversation to confirm understanding or make an inference explicit.

examples.japanese: 電車が止まっているということは、また事故があったということだ。
examples.reading: でんしゃがとまっているということは、またじこがあったということだ。
examples.english: The fact that the train has stopped means there must have been another accident.

examples.japanese: 彼が来ないということは、会議は延期ということですか。
examples.reading: かれがこないということは、かいぎはえんきということですか。
examples.english: Does the fact that he isn't coming mean the meeting is postponed?

examples.japanese: 合格したということは、これまでの努力が実を結んだということだ。
examples.reading: ごうかくしたということは、これまでのどりょくがみをむすんだということだ。
examples.english: Passing means that all the effort up to now has paid off.

title: ものだから
meaning: Because, The reason is
explanation: Grammar explanation for ものだから

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Unless you do — strong negative prerequisite
meaning: Expresses that without doing something, a certain result is impossible — stronger than a simple conditional.
explanation: ないことには～ない uses the negative plain form plus ことには to set an absolute condition: without the action in the first clause, the outcome in the second clause (also negative) cannot occur. It is more emphatic than なければ and strongly implies the first action is a non-negotiable prerequisite. The register is neutral to slightly formal, common in both speech and writing.

examples.japanese: 実際に試してみないことには、うまくいくかどうか分からない。
examples.reading: じっさいにためしてみないことには、うまくいくかどうかわからない。
examples.english: Unless you actually try it, you won't know whether it will work or not.

examples.japanese: 現場を自分の目で見ないことには、正しい判断はできない。
examples.reading: げんばをじぶんのめでみないことには、ただしいはんだんはできない。
examples.english: Unless you see the site with your own eyes, you can't make the right judgment.

examples.japanese: 本人が話してくれないことには、何があったのか分からない。
examples.reading: ほんにんがはなしてくれないことには、なにがあったのかわからない。
examples.english: Unless the person themselves tells us, we won't know what happened.

title: In order to / For the purpose of
meaning: Marks a goal, purpose, or necessary condition — what is required or appropriate in order to achieve something.
explanation: には follows a verb in dictionary form or a noun to indicate the purpose for which something is done or the condition under which something applies. When it follows a verb, it often means 'in order to do X' and is used to introduce what is necessary or expected. It can also mark a topic from a particular perspective ('for X, ...'). The register is neutral.

examples.japanese: 健康を保つには、毎日適度な運動が必要だ。
examples.reading: けんこうをたもつには、まいにちてきどなうんどうがひつようだ。
examples.english: In order to stay healthy, moderate exercise every day is necessary.

examples.japanese: このプロジェクトを成功させるには、チーム全員の協力が欠かせない。
examples.reading: このプロジェクトをせいこうさせるには、チームぜんいんのきょうりょくがかかせない。
examples.english: To make this project a success, the cooperation of the entire team is essential.

examples.japanese: 子どもには少し難しすぎる内容かもしれない。
examples.reading: こどもにはすこしむずかしすぎるないようかもしれない。
examples.english: This content might be a little too difficult for children.

title: Depends on (次第だ・次第で)
meaning: Means 'it depends on X' or 'depending on X', expressing that an outcome is determined by a particular factor.
explanation: 次第だ follows a noun and explains that the result of a situation is entirely contingent on that factor. 次第で is used mid-sentence when the dependent clause follows. This pattern is common in both spoken and written Japanese and is more formal than によって in many contexts. It places clear responsibility on the stated variable.

examples.japanese: 成功するかどうかは、あなたの努力次第だ。
examples.reading: せいこうするかどうかは、あなたのどりょくしだいだ。
examples.english: Whether you succeed or not depends entirely on your effort.

examples.japanese: やり方次第で、結果は大きく変わる。
examples.reading: やりかたしだいで、けっかはおおきくかわる。
examples.english: Depending on how you do it, the results can change dramatically.

examples.japanese: 天気次第で、ピクニックに行くかどうか決めましょう。
examples.reading: てんきしだいで、ピクニックにいくかどうかきめましょう。
examples.english: Let's decide whether to go for a picnic depending on the weather.

title: Even so / Nevertheless
meaning: Acknowledges a prior point but expresses surprise, dissatisfaction, or a further remark that goes beyond it.
explanation: それにしても is a sentence-initial expression that accepts what was just said and then pivots to an additional comment — often one of mild surprise, criticism, or emphasis. It is conversational in tone and very common in spoken Japanese. It signals that the speaker finds the situation noteworthy despite already knowing the background.

examples.japanese: 残業が多いのはわかるけど、それにしても毎日12時まで働くのは体に悪い。
examples.reading: ざんぎょうがおおいのはわかるけど、それにしてもまいにちじゅうにじまではたらくのはからだにわるい。
examples.english: I understand there's a lot of overtime, but even so, working until midnight every day is bad for your health.

examples.japanese: 彼女が忙しいのは知っているが、それにしても連絡が遅すぎる。
examples.reading: かのじょがいそがしいのはしっているが、それにしてもれんらくがおそすぎる。
examples.english: I know she's busy, but even so, her replies are way too slow.

examples.japanese: 試験が難しかったのはわかる。それにしても、全員が不合格とは驚いた。
examples.reading: しけんがむずかしかったのはわかる。それにしても、ぜんいんがふごうかくとはおどろいた。
examples.english: I understand the exam was hard. Even so, I was surprised that everyone failed.

title: Concessive acknowledgment 確かに
meaning: Used to acknowledge a point as true or valid, often before adding a contrasting opinion.
explanation: 確かに concedes that something is indeed the case, functioning like 'admittedly' or 'certainly' in English. It is neutral in register and works in both spoken and written Japanese. It frequently appears in debate, argument, or reasoning contexts, often followed by a contrasting clause with が or けど.

examples.japanese: 確かに彼の言うことは正しいが、現実的ではない。
examples.reading: たしかにかれのいうことはただしいが、げんじつてきではない。
examples.english: Certainly, what he says is correct, but it's not realistic.

examples.japanese: 確かにその映画は面白かったけど、少し長すぎると思う。
examples.reading: たしかにそのえいがはおもしろかったけど、すこしながすぎるとおもう。
examples.english: Admittedly, that movie was interesting, but I think it was a bit too long.

examples.japanese: 確かに値段は高いが、品質を考えると妥当だろう。
examples.reading: たしかにねだんはたかいが、ひんしつをかんがえるとだとうだろう。
examples.english: Certainly the price is high, but considering the quality, it's probably fair.

title: Only by doing — true value condition
meaning: Expresses that a positive result or true value is only achieved through a specific action or condition.
explanation: てこそ attaches to the て-form of a verb and emphasizes that the action is the essential condition for something meaningful to follow — similar to 'only by doing X can you truly Y.' It carries a slightly emphatic, often philosophical tone and is more common in written or formal speech. It often appears with words like 初めて (for the first time) or わかる (to understand).

examples.japanese: 苦労してこそ、成功の喜びが分かるのだ。
examples.reading: くろうしてこそ、せいこうのよろこびがわかるのだ。
examples.english: Only by going through hardship can you truly understand the joy of success.

examples.japanese: 実際に現地に行ってこそ、その文化の深さが理解できる。
examples.reading: じっさいにげんちにいってこそ、そのぶんかのふかさがりかいできる。
examples.english: Only by actually going there can you understand the depth of that culture.

examples.japanese: チームが協力してこそ、このプロジェクトは成功する。
examples.reading: チームがきょうりょくしてこそ、このプロジェクトはせいこうする。
examples.english: This project will only succeed if the team works together.

title: てならない
meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
explanation: Grammar explanation for てならない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Entrusting to someone/something
meaning: To leave something up to someone or something, or to entrust a matter to another person or force.
explanation: This expression conveys delegation or surrender of control — either to a person (上司に任せる) or to an abstract force (運命に任せる). It is neutral in register and appears in both spoken and written Japanese. Use it when the speaker consciously hands over responsibility or allows something to proceed without interference.

examples.japanese: 細かい計画は部下に任せることにした。
examples.reading: こまかいけいかくはぶかにまかせることにした。
examples.english: I decided to leave the detailed planning up to my subordinates.

examples.japanese: この件の判断は専門家に任せた方がいい。
examples.reading: このけんのはんだんはせんもんかにまかせたほうがいい。
examples.english: It's better to leave the decision on this matter to an expert.

examples.japanese: 彼は結果を運に任せて、思い切って挑戦した。
examples.reading: かれはけっかをうんにまかせて、おもいきってちょうせんした。
examples.english: He left the outcome to chance and boldly took on the challenge.

title: Now that / Since one has
meaning: Expresses that given a certain situation or decision, a natural obligation or course of action follows.
explanation: 上は attaches to the plain form of a verb and conveys the sense of 'now that X is the case, one must/should do Y'. It implies that because a decision has been made or a situation is set, there is no going back and the speaker accepts the resulting responsibility or consequence. It is formal and more common in written Japanese or serious spoken contexts.

examples.japanese: 引き受けた上は、最後まで責任を持ってやり遂げます。
examples.reading: ひきうけたうえは、さいごまでせきにんをもってやりとげます。
examples.english: Now that I've accepted the task, I will see it through to the end with full responsibility.

examples.japanese: 日本に来た上は、日本語をしっかり勉強するつもりだ。
examples.reading: にほんにきたうえは、にほんごをしっかりべんきょうするつもりだ。
examples.english: Now that I've come to Japan, I intend to study Japanese seriously.

examples.japanese: 約束した上は、どんなに大変でも守らなければならない。
examples.reading: やくそくしたうえは、どんなにたいへんでもまもらなければならない。
examples.english: Since I made a promise, I must keep it no matter how hard it is.

title: Have no choice but to
meaning: Expresses that one cannot avoid doing something; one is compelled to act.
explanation: Formed by attaching ざるを得ない to the plain negative stem of a verb (the same stem used for ない, but without ない). The する verb becomes せざるを得ない. This pattern conveys reluctant necessity — the speaker does not want to do something but has no other option. It is formal in tone and common in written and spoken formal Japanese.

examples.japanese: 電車が止まったので、タクシーで行かざるを得なかった。
examples.reading: でんしゃがとまったので、タクシーでいかざるをえなかった。
examples.english: Since the trains stopped, I had no choice but to take a taxi.

examples.japanese: 証拠がそろっている以上、彼の主張を認めざるを得ない。
examples.reading: しょうこがそろっているいじょう、かれのしゅちょうをみとめざるをえない。
examples.english: Given that all the evidence is in order, I have no choice but to accept his claim.

examples.japanese: 予算が足りなくて、プロジェクトを中止せざるを得ない状況だ。
examples.reading: よさんがたりなくて、プロジェクトをちゅうしせざるをえないじょうきょうだ。
examples.english: With the budget being insufficient, we are in a situation where we have no choice but to cancel the project.

title: だけは
meaning: To do all that one can, As much or many ~ as one could
explanation: Grammar explanation for だけは

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: To be bothered by / To have on one's mind
meaning: Expresses that something is weighing on one's mind, catching one's attention, or causing mild concern or curiosity.
explanation: が気になる describes a state where something occupies your thoughts — either because you are curious about it, worried about it, or find it distracting. The subject is the thing that is on your mind, and the experiencer is the topic (は). It is widely used in casual and polite speech alike, and carries a range from light curiosity to genuine worry depending on context.

examples.japanese: 彼が最近元気がないのが気になっている。
examples.reading: かれがさいきんげんきがないのがきになっている。
examples.english: I've been concerned that he hasn't seemed himself lately.

examples.japanese: あの店の新メニューが気になって、ずっと頭から離れない。
examples.reading: あのみせのしんメニューがきになって、ずっとあたまからはなれない。
examples.english: I can't stop thinking about that restaurant's new menu — it's really caught my attention.

examples.japanese: 試験の結果が気になって、なかなか眠れなかった。
examples.reading: しけんのけっかがきになって、なかなかねむれなかった。
examples.english: I was so anxious about the exam results that I couldn't fall asleep.

title: As a / From the standpoint of
meaning: Marks a role, position, or category as the perspective or standard from which a judgment is being made.
explanation: としては sets up a frame of reference — the subject is being evaluated or described in their capacity as something. It is commonly used to express that something is good or bad relative to a particular standard or role. The register is neutral and suitable for both written and spoken Japanese.

examples.japanese: 初心者としては、この作品はとてもよくできていると思う。
examples.reading: しょしんしゃとしては、このさくひんはとてもよくできているとおもう。
examples.english: For a beginner, I think this work is very well done.

examples.japanese: 親としては、子どもの安全が一番心配だ。
examples.reading: おやとしては、こどものあんぜんがいちばんしんぱいだ。
examples.english: As a parent, my biggest concern is my child's safety.

examples.japanese: 上司としては、部下の意見も尊重しなければならない。
examples.reading: じょうしとしては、ぶかのいけんもそんちょうしなければならない。
examples.english: As a manager, you also have to respect your subordinates' opinions.

title: Since / Now that (strong commitment)
meaning: Expresses that because a certain condition or decision holds true, a natural or obligatory consequence must follow.
explanation: からには follows a verb or adjective in plain form and establishes a strong logical link: given the premise stated before it, the speaker asserts that something must, should, or will inevitably happen. It carries a sense of resolve or moral obligation and is often used when making personal commitments or stating expectations of others. It is moderately formal and more common in spoken than written Japanese.

examples.japanese: やると決めたからには、最後まで頑張るつもりだ。
examples.reading: やるときめたからには、さいごまでがんばるつもりだ。
examples.english: Now that I've decided to do it, I intend to see it through to the end.

examples.japanese: 参加するからには、全力を尽くしてください。
examples.reading: さんかするからには、ぜんりょくをつくしてください。
examples.english: Since you're participating, please give it your all.

examples.japanese: プロとして仕事を引き受けたからには、責任を持って完成させなければならない。
examples.reading: プロとしてしごとをひきうけたからには、せきにんをもってかんせいさせなければならない。
examples.english: Now that I've taken on the job as a professional, I have to complete it responsibly.

title: Not entirely without — mild affirmation via double negative
meaning: A double negative construction meaning 'it's not that there isn't' — used to express mild agreement, possibility, or a nuanced partial affirmative.
explanation: なくはない is formed by negating an adjective or verb with なく and adding はない, creating a double negative that softens a statement — conveying 'it's not that I don't' or 'I suppose you could say.' It is used to acknowledge something as possible or true without fully committing, often expressing hedged opinions or understated emotions. The register is conversational to neutral and frequently appears in discussions where the speaker wants to be diplomatic or tentative.

examples.japanese: 彼の言いたいことは分からなくはないが、賛成はできない。
examples.reading: かれのいいたいことはわからなくはないが、さんせいはできない。
examples.english: I'm not saying I don't understand what he means, but I can't agree.

examples.japanese: もう少し時間があれば、できなくはないと思う。
examples.reading: もうすこしじかんがあれば、できなくはないとおもう。
examples.english: I think it's not entirely impossible if we have a bit more time.

examples.japanese: 正直、少し心配じゃなくはないけど、大丈夫だと信じている。
examples.reading: しょうじき、すこししんぱいじゃなくはないけど、だいじょうぶだとしんじている。
examples.english: Honestly, I'm not exactly without worry, but I believe it'll be fine.

title: ことにはならない
meaning: Just because… doesn't mean…
explanation: Grammar explanation for ことにはならない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

pattern: ないわけにはいかない
title: ないわけにはいかない
meaning: Can't not, No way to avoid
explanation: Grammar explanation for ないわけにはいかない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

bunproSlug: ないわけにはいかない

title: Tentative suggestion with negation
meaning: I think... isn't it?, It might be that..., Don't you agree?
explanation: This pattern combines the nominalizer の with ではないだろうか to express polite, tentative suggestions or to question assumptions. It softens statements and invites agreement or consideration of an alternative viewpoint. Used in both written and spoken Japanese at formal or semi-formal registers.

examples.japanese: 私たちの案が一番いいのではないだろうか。
examples.reading: わたしたちのあんがいちばんいいのではないだろうか。
examples.english: Isn't our idea the very best?

examples.japanese: 彼は来ないのではないだろうか。
examples.reading: かれはこないのではないだろうか。
examples.english: Don't you think he won't come?

examples.japanese: この方法が正しいのではないだろうか。
examples.reading: このほうほうがただしいのではないだろうか。
examples.english: Might this method be the correct one?

title: ものがある
meaning: Strong judgement, There is something, Feel strongly that
explanation: Grammar explanation for ものがある

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: 何といっても
meaning: By any account, All the same, No doubt
explanation: Grammar explanation for 何といっても

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Classical location/means marker (にて)
meaning: A formal or classical particle indicating location, means, or manner, equivalent to で in modern Japanese.
explanation: にて is the classical counterpart of the modern particle で, used to mark the place where something happens, the means or instrument used, or the manner of an action. It is found in formal announcements, official documents, invitations, and written notices. While で is used in everyday speech, にて gives a stiff, ceremonial tone appropriate for formal contexts.

examples.japanese: 本日は東京国際フォーラムにて、授賞式が行われます。
examples.reading: ほんじつはとうきょうこくさいフォーラムにて、じゅしょうしきがおこなわれます。
examples.english: Today, the award ceremony will be held at the Tokyo International Forum.

examples.japanese: 詳細につきましては、メールにてご連絡いたします。
examples.reading: しょうさいにつきましては、メールにてごれんらくいたします。
examples.english: We will contact you with further details via email.

examples.japanese: 応募はウェブサイトにてお受けしております。
examples.reading: おうぼはウェブサイトにておうけしております。
examples.english: We are accepting applications through our website.

title: それなのに
meaning: And yet, Despite this
explanation: Grammar explanation for それなのに

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Just before / From one's own standpoint
meaning: Means either 'just before (a location)' in a physical sense, or 'given one's position/pride', implying obligation due to social face.
explanation: 手前 has two related uses: (1) physically, it means 'just before' or 'in front of' a landmark; (2) abstractly, it expresses that due to one's own position, past statements, or social obligations, one is compelled to act in a certain way ('given what I said, I can't back out now'). The second use carries a nuance of personal dignity or face. Both uses are common in everyday and formal Japanese.

examples.japanese: 駅の手前に大きなスーパーがあります。
examples.reading: えきのてまえにおおきなスーパーがあります。
examples.english: There is a large supermarket just before the station.

examples.japanese: 自分で言い出した手前、途中でやめるわけにはいかない。
examples.reading: じぶんでいいだしたてまえ、とちゅうでやめるわけにはいかない。
examples.english: Since I was the one who brought it up, I can't quit halfway through.

examples.japanese: みんなの手前、失敗は見せられないというプレッシャーを感じた。
examples.reading: みんなのてまえ、しっぱいはみせられないというプレッシャーをかんじた。
examples.english: I felt pressure not to show any failure in front of everyone.

title: てたまらない
meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
explanation: Grammar explanation for てたまらない

examples.japanese: 例文です。

title: といった
meaning: Such as, Like, So much as
explanation: Grammar explanation for といった

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Was about to / narrowly avoided
meaning: Expresses that something almost happened but was narrowly avoided, often with a sense of relief or alarm.
explanation: This usage of ところだった (as opposed to the neutral 'was in the middle of') focuses on near-misses. It attaches to the plain non-past form of a verb and indicates a dangerous or undesirable event that was barely avoided. The nuance is retrospective — the speaker reflects on what could have gone wrong. Used in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: あと少しで車にひかれるところだった。
examples.reading: あとすこしでくるまにひかれるところだった。
examples.english: I was almost run over by a car.

examples.japanese: うっかり大事な書類を捨てるところだった。
examples.reading: うっかりだいじなしょるいをすてるところだった。
examples.english: I nearly threw away an important document by mistake.

examples.japanese: 寝過ごして、試験に遅刻するところだった。
examples.reading: ねすごして、しけんにちこくするところだった。
examples.english: I overslept and almost arrived late to the exam.

title: ようでは・ようじゃ
meaning: If, If it is true that
explanation: Grammar explanation for ようでは・ようじゃ

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

pattern: ものですから・もので
title: ものですから・もので
meaning: Because, My excuse is, My explanation is
explanation: Grammar explanation for ものですから・もので

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Furthermore / On top of that
meaning: A conjunctive expression meaning 'in addition' or 'furthermore', used to add a second point that reinforces or compounds the first.
explanation: その上 connects two clauses or sentences, introducing additional information that strengthens the preceding statement, often piling on a second negative or positive quality. It is slightly more formal than それに or しかも and is common in both written and spoken contexts. The added point typically goes in the same direction as the first (both positive or both negative).

examples.japanese: 彼女は仕事が速い。その上、ミスもほとんどない。
examples.reading: かのじょはしごとがはやい。そのうえ、ミスもほとんどない。
examples.english: She works quickly. On top of that, she almost never makes mistakes.

examples.japanese: この部屋は狭い。その上、日当たりも悪い。
examples.reading: このへやはせまい。そのうえ、ひあたりもわるい。
examples.english: This room is small. Furthermore, it gets poor sunlight.

examples.japanese: 試験に落ちてしまった。その上、財布まで失くしてしまった。
examples.reading: しけんにおちてしまった。そのうえ、さいふまでなくしてしまった。
examples.english: I failed the exam. On top of that, I even lost my wallet.

title: Cannot help doing — irresistible impulse
meaning: Expresses that one cannot stop oneself from doing something — an action or feeling that is unavoidable or irresistible.
explanation: ないではいられない attaches to the negative stem of a verb (ない form minus い) and conveys that the speaker is compelled to do something despite potentially not wanting to, or that a feeling or reaction is impossible to suppress. It expresses strong involuntary impulse and is similar to 'can't help but' in English. The register is neutral and works in both spoken and written Japanese; the colloquial form ずにはいられない is interchangeable.

examples.japanese: その映画を見て、泣かないではいられなかった。
examples.reading: そのえいがをみて、なかないではいられなかった。
examples.english: After watching that film, I couldn't help but cry.

examples.japanese: 彼女の冗談を聞いて、笑わないではいられない。
examples.reading: かのじょのじょうだんをきいて、わらわないではいられない。
examples.english: Hearing her jokes, I can't help but laugh.

examples.japanese: 不公平な扱いを受けたとき、抗議しないではいられなかった。
examples.reading: ふこうへいなあつかいをうけたとき、こうぎしないではいられなかった。
examples.english: When I was treated unfairly, I couldn't help but protest.

title: だけましだ
meaning: At least, It's better than..., One should feel grateful for
explanation: Grammar explanation for だけましだ

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: That's what ~ is / Speaker's conclusion or criticism
meaning: Expresses the speaker's strong conclusion, criticism, or impression about what something truly is or ought to be.
explanation: というものだ is used to assert the speaker's view about the essential nature of something — either praising it as the ideal or criticizing it as unacceptable. It often carries emotional weight and can express admiration, reproach, or strong conviction. The register is neutral to slightly formal and often appears in monologue or essay-style speech.

examples.japanese: 友達が困っているときに助けるのが、本当の友情というものだ。
examples.reading: ともだちがこまっているときにたすけるのが、ほんとうのゆうじょうというものだ。
examples.english: Helping a friend when they're in trouble — that's what true friendship is.

examples.japanese: 人の話を最後まで聞かないのは、失礼というものだ。
examples.reading: ひとのはなしをさいごまできかないのは、しつれいというものだ。
examples.english: Not listening to someone until the end — that is simply rude.

examples.japanese: 長年かけて完成させた作品を一言で否定されるのは、悲しいというものだ。
examples.reading: ながねんかけてかんせいさせたさくひんをひとことでひていされるのは、かなしいというものだ。
examples.english: Having a work you spent years completing dismissed in a single word — that is truly heartbreaking.

title: Rhetorical invitation: Let's do this
meaning: A rhetorical expression used to call on others to take action together, often with a sense of encouragement or rallying.
explanation: ようではないか attaches to the volitional stem of a verb and functions as a stirring, often formal invitation for collective action. It is similar to ようではありませんか in meaning but more direct and impassioned. This pattern is often found in speeches, motivational contexts, or literary writing and carries a slightly formal or dramatic tone.

examples.japanese: 困難に立ち向かい、共に未来を切り開こうではないか。
examples.reading: こんなんにたちむかい、ともにみらいをきりひらこうではないか。
examples.english: Let us face these difficulties together and forge a path to the future.

examples.japanese: この問題について、もっと真剣に考えようではないか。
examples.reading: このもんだいについて、もっとしんけんにかんがえようではないか。
examples.english: Why don't we think more seriously about this issue?

examples.japanese: もう一度チャレンジしてみようではないか。
examples.reading: もういちどチャレンジしてみようではないか。
examples.english: Let's give it another try, shall we?

title: No time for / far from / out of the question
meaning: Strongly asserts that something is impossible, inappropriate, or out of the question given the current circumstances.
explanation: Attaches to nouns or the plain form of verbs to express that the situation is so extreme that the mentioned thing cannot even be considered. It carries a strong dismissive or exasperated tone. Used in both spoken and written Japanese; often follows a clause that explains why the situation is so pressing or dire.

examples.japanese: 締め切りが明日なので、遊んでいるどころではない。
examples.reading: しめきりがあしたなので、あそんでいるどころではない。
examples.english: The deadline is tomorrow, so this is no time to be playing around.

examples.japanese: 熱が39度もあるから、仕事どころではない。
examples.reading: ねつが39どもあるから、しごとどころではない。
examples.english: I have a fever of 39 degrees — work is out of the question.

examples.japanese: 財布を落として、旅行を楽しむどころではなかった。
examples.reading: さいふをおとして、りょこうをたのしむどころではなかった。
examples.english: I had lost my wallet, so enjoying the trip was far from possible.

title: In a ~ way / In a manner that suggests
meaning: Describes the manner or style in which something is done, often implying it resembles a certain quality or gives a certain impression.
explanation: ふう (風) means 'style' or 'manner' and when followed by に, it functions as an adverb meaning 'in a ~ way' or 'in a way that looks like.' It is used to describe how something appears or is done, often with a nuance of resemblance or impression rather than fact. The register is casual to neutral.

examples.japanese: 彼はいかにも慣れているふうに話していた。
examples.reading: かれはいかにもなれているふうにはなしていた。
examples.english: He was speaking in a way that suggested he was very experienced.

examples.japanese: 先生は何も知らないふうに振る舞っていたが、実は全部わかっていた。
examples.reading: せんせいはなにもしらないふうにふるまっていたが、じつはぜんぶわかっていた。
examples.english: The teacher was acting as though they knew nothing, but in fact understood everything.

examples.japanese: 外国人ふうに話すと、もっと印象的に聞こえる場合もある。
examples.reading: がいこくじんふうにはなすと、もっとさんしょうてきにきこえるばあいもある。
examples.english: Speaking in a foreign-influenced style can sometimes sound more impressive.

title: Even…, Judging from the very start
meaning: Highlights the most basic or obvious example to support a broader conclusion, or signals a judgment based on a single telling detail.
explanation: からして attaches to a noun that represents either the most fundamental or the most telling element of something, implying 'even this baseline detail points to the conclusion.' It is often used to criticize or to emphasize that something is problematic at its most basic level. Register is neutral to slightly informal.

examples.japanese: 彼女は話し方からして、自信に満ちている。
examples.reading: かのじょははなしかたからして、じしんにみちている。
examples.english: Even the way she speaks shows she is full of confidence.

examples.japanese: その店は外観からして、あまり清潔そうではない。
examples.reading: そのみせはがいかんからして、あまりせいけつそうではない。
examples.english: Judging from its appearance alone, that restaurant doesn't look very clean.

examples.japanese: 彼の計画は出発点からして間違っていた。
examples.reading: かれのけいかくはしゅっぱつてんからしてまちがっていた。
examples.english: His plan was wrong from the very starting point.

title: Judging from what I see
meaning: Used to draw a conclusion or make an inference based on observable evidence.
explanation: ところを見ると attaches to a plain-form verb or adjective clause describing something you have witnessed, then introduces your inference. It implies the speaker is reasoning from direct observation rather than hearsay. Register is neutral and works in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: 彼女が泣いているところを見ると、何か悪いことがあったに違いない。
examples.reading: かのじょがないているところをみると、なにかわるいことがあったにちがいない。
examples.english: Judging from the fact that she is crying, something bad must have happened.

examples.japanese: 二人が笑いながら話しているところを見ると、仲直りしたようだ。
examples.reading: ふたりがわらいながらはなしているところをみると、なかなおりしたようだ。
examples.english: Seeing that the two of them are talking and laughing, it seems they made up.

examples.japanese: 彼がそんなに急いでいるところを見ると、電車に乗り遅れそうなのだろう。
examples.reading: かれがそんなにいそいでいるところをみると、でんしゃにのりおくれそうなのだろう。
examples.english: Judging from how much he's rushing, he's probably about to miss his train.

pattern: かと思ったら・かと思うと
title: Just when I thought / No sooner than
meaning: Expresses that no sooner had one thing happened (or appeared to happen) than something else — often surprising or contrasting — occurred immediately after.
explanation: かと思ったら (past) and かと思うと (present/general) follow a verb in plain form and indicate that a second, often unexpected event followed the first almost instantly. They convey a sense of surprise at the speed or the contrast of events. The pattern is mostly used in spoken and casual written Japanese and often carries a slightly exasperated or astonished nuance.

examples.japanese: 雨が止んだかと思ったら、またすぐに降り始めた。
examples.reading: あめがやんだかとおもったら、またすぐにふりはじめた。
examples.english: Just when I thought the rain had stopped, it started again right away.

examples.japanese: 子どもは泣いていたかと思うと、急に笑い出した。
examples.reading: こどもはないていたかとおもうと、きゅうにわらいだした。
examples.english: No sooner had the child been crying than they suddenly burst out laughing.

examples.japanese: 彼は会議室に入ってきたかと思ったら、すぐに出ていってしまった。
examples.reading: かれはかいぎしつにはいってきたかとおもったら、すぐにでていってしまった。
examples.english: Just when I thought he had come into the meeting room, he left immediately.

bunproSlug: かと思ったら-かと思うと

title: 気
meaning: Feel like ~, Spirit
explanation: Grammar explanation for 気

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Without doing / Without ever
meaning: Expresses that an action is performed completely without the occurrence of another action, often emphasizing continuity or perseverance.
explanation: ことなく attaches to the dictionary form of a verb and means the subject does something without ever doing the action named. It has a more formal and literary tone than ずに or ないで, and is more common in written Japanese. It often implies praise or emphasis on the absence of interruption, hesitation, or fault.

examples.japanese: 彼女は一度も休むことなく、マラソンを完走した。
examples.reading: かのじょはいちどもやすむことなく、マラソンをかんそうした。
examples.english: She completed the marathon without resting even once.

examples.japanese: その職人は文句を言うことなく、黙々と仕事を続けた。
examples.reading: そのしょくにんはもんくをいうことなく、もくもくとしごとをつづけた。
examples.english: The craftsman continued working in silence without a word of complaint.

examples.japanese: 患者のプライバシーを侵すことなく、適切な情報を提供することが求められる。
examples.reading: かんじゃのプライバシーをおかすことなく、てきせつなじょうほうをていきょうすることがもとめられる。
examples.english: It is required to provide appropriate information without violating patient privacy.

title: Through / Amid (adverse conditions)
meaning: Through, amid, or in the face of — used to emphasize that an action takes place inside or through a difficult or noteworthy surrounding condition.
explanation: Noun + 中を highlights the conditions under which an action occurs, often stressing that the action was carried out despite or within those conditions (e.g., 雨の中を, 忙しい中を). The particle を marks the conditions as a 'space traversed' or endured, lending a sense of effort or persistence. It is more expressive and literary than で and is common in formal speech and writing.

examples.japanese: 大雨の中を、彼は傘もささずに走ってきた。
examples.reading: おおあめのなかを、かれはかさもささずにはしってきた。
examples.english: He came running through the heavy rain without even opening an umbrella.

examples.japanese: お忙しい中をお越しいただき、ありがとうございます。
examples.reading: おいそがしいなかをおこしいただき、ありがとうございます。
examples.english: Thank you very much for coming despite your busy schedule.

examples.japanese: 寒い中を長時間並んでくれたファンに感謝している。
examples.reading: さむいなかをちょうじかんならんでくれたファンにかんしゃしている。
examples.english: I am grateful to the fans who stood in line for hours in the cold.

title: Of all times / Only when (に限って)
meaning: Expresses ironic frustration that something happens specifically with a particular person or at a particular time.
explanation: に限って follows a noun and conveys the speaker's surprise or mild exasperation that the situation applies precisely to that person or moment — often when it is least expected or most inconvenient. It is used in everyday spoken Japanese with an ironic or emphatic nuance, and is not interchangeable with だけ.

examples.japanese: 自分の子に限って、そんなことはしないと思っていた。
examples.reading: じぶんのこにかぎって、そんなことはしないとおもっていた。
examples.english: I thought that, of all children, my own would never do such a thing.

examples.japanese: 急いでいるときに限って、電車が遅れる。
examples.reading: いそいでいるときにかぎって、でんしゃがおくれる。
examples.english: Of all times, it's always when I'm in a hurry that the train is late.

examples.japanese: 大事な試合に限って、雨が降るんだよな。
examples.reading: だいじなしあいにかぎって、あめがふるんだよな。
examples.english: It only ever rains on the days of important matches.

title: Consequential conjunction それなら
meaning: Draws a logical consequence or response based on what was just said, equivalent to 'in that case' or 'if so.'
explanation: それなら is a conjunctive expression meaning 'if that is the case' or 'then,' used to transition from what was just stated to a conclusion, suggestion, or decision that follows logically. It is casual to neutral in register and very common in conversation. It differs from じゃあ in that it more explicitly references the prior statement as the condition for what follows.

examples.japanese: 「明日は休みです。」「それなら、一緒に出かけましょう。」
examples.reading: 「あしたはやすみです。」「それなら、いっしょにでかけましょう。」
examples.english: 'I'm off tomorrow.' 'In that case, let's go out together.'

examples.japanese: 車がないんですね。それなら、駅まで送りますよ。
examples.reading: くるまがないんですね。それなら、えきまでおくりますよ。
examples.english: You don't have a car? In that case, I'll give you a ride to the station.

examples.japanese: もう準備ができているなら、それなら早めに出発しましょう。
examples.reading: もうじゅんびができているなら、それなら、はやめにしゅっぱつしましょう。
examples.english: If you're already ready, then let's leave a bit early.

title: てしょうがない
meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
explanation: Grammar explanation for てしょうがない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Natural consequence
meaning: It is natural that, As a matter of course
explanation: The て-form linked with 当然だ expresses that something is a natural result or expected outcome. Often used with a reason or cause before the て-form to explain why an outcome is inevitable or expected. Common in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: 毎日練習しているのだから上達して当然だ。
examples.reading: まいにちれんしゅうしているのだからじょうたつしてとうぜんだ。
examples.english: Because you practice every day, improvement is only natural.

examples.japanese: 長時間働いているから疲れて当然だ。
examples.reading: ながじかんはたらいているからつかれてとうぜんだ。
examples.english: Because you work long hours, it's natural to be tired.

examples.japanese: 新しいシステムだから使い方がわからなくて当然だ。
examples.reading: あたらしいしすてむだからつかいかたがわからなくてとうぜんだ。
examples.english: Since it's a new system, it's natural not to understand how to use it.

pattern: ようがない・ようもない
title: Impossibility of method ようがない
meaning: Expresses that there is no possible way to do something, as no means or method exists.
explanation: ようがない and its softer variant ようもない attach to the verb stem (masu-stem) to indicate that doing something is completely impossible because there is no available means or method. This is different from できない in that it emphasizes the absence of any way, not just inability. It is used in both spoken and written Japanese and carries a tone of resignation or frustration.

examples.japanese: 彼女の電話番号を知らないので、連絡のしようがない。
examples.reading: かのじょのでんわばんごうをしらないので、れんらくのしようがない。
examples.english: I don't know her phone number, so there's no way to contact her.

examples.japanese: 証拠がなければ、信じようがないよ。
examples.reading: しょうこがなければ、しんじようがないよ。
examples.english: Without evidence, there's no way I can believe it.

examples.japanese: 説明書が全部フランス語で、読みようもなかった。
examples.reading: せつめいしょがぜんぶフランスごで、よみようもなかった。
examples.english: The manual was entirely in French, so there was no way to read it.

title: Anyway / it'll end up that way
meaning: Expresses resignation or certainty that something will turn out a certain (usually negative) way regardless of effort.
explanation: どうせ is an adverb that conveys the speaker's resigned or fatalistic attitude — that the outcome is inevitable no matter what one does. It often implies pessimism or sarcasm ('it's going to be that way anyway, so why bother'). It is used in casual to neutral speech and is rarely found in formal writing. It frequently pairs with んだから, だから, or similar expressions of reasoning.

examples.japanese: どうせ試験に落ちると思って、あまり勉強しなかった。
examples.reading: どうせしけんにおちるとおもって、あまりべんきょうしなかった。
examples.english: Thinking I'd fail the exam anyway, I didn't study much.

examples.japanese: どうせ誰も来ないんだから、パーティーを中止にしよう。
examples.reading: どうせだれもこないんだから、パーティーをちゅうしにしよう。
examples.english: Since nobody is going to come anyway, let's cancel the party.

examples.japanese: どうせまた同じミスを繰り返すに決まっている。
examples.reading: どうせまたおなじミスをくりかえすにきまっている。
examples.english: They'll definitely just repeat the same mistake again anyway.

title: いきなり
meaning: For no reason, Suddenly, All of a sudden
explanation: Grammar explanation for いきなり

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Just because
meaning: Used to warn that a stated reason or condition does not automatically justify a particular conclusion or action.
explanation: からといって introduces a premise that might seem like a valid reason, then immediately refutes or qualifies it, often with a negative or cautionary statement in the following clause. It is equivalent to 'just because X doesn't mean Y.' Register is neutral and the pattern is common in both conversation and formal writing.

examples.japanese: 忙しいからといって、健康を無視していいわけではない。
examples.reading: いそがしいからといって、けんこうをむししていいわけではない。
examples.english: Just because you're busy doesn't mean you can ignore your health.

examples.japanese: お金があるからといって、何でも買えるわけではない。
examples.reading: おかねがあるからといって、なんでもかえるわけではない。
examples.english: Just because you have money doesn't mean you can buy everything.

examples.japanese: 失敗したからといって、すぐに諦める必要はない。
examples.reading: しっぱいしたからといって、すぐにあきらめるひつようはない。
examples.english: Just because you failed doesn't mean you need to give up right away.

title: ことは〜が
meaning: It is true...but, although, but
explanation: Grammar explanation for ことは〜が

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Emphatic negation
meaning: Definitely not, absolutely not, impossible, do you really think~?
explanation: The nominalizer もの combined with the question particle か creates a strong emphatic negation or rhetorical question. It expresses the speaker's firm conviction that something will not or cannot happen, often with emotional intensity. Common in both written and spoken Japanese.

examples.japanese: そんなことができるものか。
examples.reading: そんなことができるものか。
examples.english: As if I could do such a thing!

examples.japanese: 彼がそんなことをするものか。
examples.reading: かれがそんなことをするものか。
examples.english: There's no way he would do such a thing.

examples.japanese: 私がそんなに簡単にあきらめるものか。
examples.reading: わたしがそんなにかんたんにあきらめるものか。
examples.english: Do you really think I would give up so easily?

title: Not only… but also (に限らず)
meaning: Means 'not only X but also', extending a statement beyond a single subject to a broader group.
explanation: に限らず follows a noun and expands the scope of the sentence, indicating that the statement is not restricted to the mentioned item alone. It is often paired with も or だけでなく to strengthen the expansive meaning. Common in both spoken and written Japanese, it is slightly more formal than だけじゃなくて.

examples.japanese: 日本人に限らず、外国人も多くこの祭りに参加する。
examples.reading: にほんじんにかぎらず、がいこくじんもおおくこのまつりにさんかする。
examples.english: Not only Japanese people, but many foreigners also take part in this festival.

examples.japanese: 子どもに限らず、大人もこのゲームを楽しんでいる。
examples.reading: こどもにかぎらず、おとなもこのゲームをたのしんでいる。
examples.english: Not just children, but adults too are enjoying this game.

examples.japanese: スポーツに限らず、音楽や美術の才能も大切だ。
examples.reading: スポーツにかぎらず、おんがくやびじゅつのさいのうもたいせつだ。
examples.english: Talent in music and art is also important, not just talent in sports.

title: 更に
meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
explanation: Grammar explanation for 更に

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Or / or else (presenting alternatives)
meaning: Used to present an alternative option, especially in questions or when asking someone to choose between two possibilities.
explanation: それとも is a conjunction that connects two alternatives, similar to 'or' or 'or else' in English. It is most naturally used in questions where the speaker asks the listener to choose between options. Unlike か, which is a simple disjunction, それとも implies the alternatives are somewhat contrasting or mutually exclusive. It is conversational and common in spoken Japanese.

examples.japanese: コーヒーにしますか、それとも紅茶にしますか。
examples.reading: コーヒーにしますか、それともこうちゃにしますか。
examples.english: Would you like coffee, or rather tea?

examples.japanese: 電車で行きますか、それともバスで行きますか。
examples.reading: でんしゃでいきますか、それともバスでいきますか。
examples.english: Are you going by train, or by bus?

examples.japanese: 自分でやってみますか、それとも手伝いましょうか。
examples.reading: じぶんでやってみますか、それともてつだいましょうか。
examples.english: Do you want to try it yourself, or shall I help you?

title: Not until after doing — sequential prerequisite
meaning: Expresses that a second action cannot happen unless or until the first action is completed.
explanation: てからでないと (or てからでなければ) attaches to the て-form of a verb and sets up a strict prerequisite: the result in the main clause (usually negative) cannot occur without first completing the action in the subordinate clause. It is more emphatic than てから alone and is used in both spoken and written Japanese at a fairly neutral register. The main clause typically ends with できない, わからない, or a similar negative expression.

examples.japanese: 契約書にサインしてからでないと、作業を始めることができません。
examples.reading: けいやくしょにサインしてからでないと、さぎょうをはじめることができません。
examples.english: We cannot begin work until after you have signed the contract.

examples.japanese: 実際に使ってみてからでないと、その製品の良さは分からない。
examples.reading: じっさいにつかってみてからでないと、そのせいひんのよさはわからない。
examples.english: You won't know how good that product is until you actually try using it.

examples.japanese: 上司に確認してからでないと、返事できません。
examples.reading: じょうしにかくにんしてからでないと、へんじできません。
examples.english: I cannot give you an answer until I check with my supervisor.

title: On the way / In the middle of
meaning: On the way to somewhere, partway through a journey, or in the middle of an activity or process.
explanation: 途中に focuses on a point in space or time ('at a point along the way'), while 途中で focuses on an action or interruption ('in the middle of doing'). Both are natural and neutral in register. 途中で is especially common when describing something that happened or was done before completion, often implying interruption or a pause mid-process.

examples.japanese: 学校への途中で、財布を忘れたことに気がついた。
examples.reading: がっこうへのとちゅうで、さいふをわすれたことにきがついた。
examples.english: On the way to school, I realized I had forgotten my wallet.

examples.japanese: 映画の途中で眠くなって、結末が見られなかった。
examples.reading: えいがのとちゅうでねむくなって、けつまつがみられなかった。
examples.english: I got sleepy in the middle of the movie and couldn't see the ending.

examples.japanese: 駅への途中に、新しいカフェができていた。
examples.reading: えきへのとちゅうに、あたらしいカフェができていた。
examples.english: On the way to the station, a new café had opened.

title: Not necessarily so
meaning: Indicates that something is not always true or not guaranteed, pushing back against a simplistic or absolute assumption.
explanation: This expression attaches to a noun or plain verb form and is used to caution that a particular outcome or rule does not always apply. It implies nuance — that the situation is more complex than a straightforward assumption suggests. It is neutral to slightly formal in register and often appears in written or analytical speech.

examples.japanese: 高ければいいというものでもない。品質をよく確認すべきだ。
examples.reading: たかければいいというものでもない。ひんしつをよくかくにんすべきだ。
examples.english: Just because something is expensive doesn't mean it's good. You should check the quality carefully.

examples.japanese: 謝ればすべて解決するというものでもない。
examples.reading: あやまればすべてかいけつするというものでもない。
examples.english: There's no guarantee that apologizing will resolve everything.

examples.japanese: 努力すれば必ず成功するというものでもないが、諦めなければ可能性は広がる。
examples.reading: どりょくすればかならずせいこうするというものでもないが、あきらめなければかのうせいはひろがる。
examples.english: It's not necessarily true that hard work always leads to success, but if you don't give up, the possibilities expand.

title: Timing: just when / right at the moment
meaning: Expresses that something happens at a specific, often significant or inconvenient moment.
explanation: Attaches to the plain present or past form of a verb to indicate that a second event occurs right at that moment. ところに tends to focus on the timing itself, while ところへ adds a nuance of something arriving or intruding into that moment. Used in both spoken and written Japanese; the resulting sentence often carries a sense of surprise, coincidence, or disruption.

examples.japanese: 出かけようとしていたところに、友達から電話がかかってきた。
examples.reading: でかけようとしていたところに、ともだちからでんわがかかってきた。
examples.english: Just as I was about to head out, I got a call from a friend.

examples.japanese: 困っているところへ、先生が助けに来てくれた。
examples.reading: こまっているところへ、せんせいがたすけにきてくれた。
examples.english: Right when I was in trouble, my teacher came to help.

examples.japanese: やっと眠れたところに、隣の部屋で騒ぎ出した。
examples.reading: やっとねむれたところに、となりのへやでさわぎだした。
examples.english: Just when I had finally fallen asleep, the people next door started making noise.

title: とも
meaning: Even if, No matter
explanation: Grammar explanation for とも

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: In short — summarizing conjunction
meaning: Used to introduce a summary or simplified restatement of what was just said.
explanation: 要するに is a conjunction meaning 'in short,' 'to sum up,' or 'in other words,' and is placed at the start of a clause to signal that the speaker is distilling a previous explanation into its key point. It is neutral to slightly formal in register and is common in both spoken and written Japanese, especially in explanations, reports, or discussions. It often appears after a long explanation to deliver the bottom line.

examples.japanese: 色々と説明しましたが、要するに今月中に提出してください。
examples.reading: いろいろとせつめいしましたが、ようするにこんげつちゅうにていしゅつしてください。
examples.english: I explained various things, but in short, please submit it by the end of this month.

examples.japanese: 要するに、彼はその仕事をやりたくないということだ。
examples.reading: ようするに、かれはそのしごとをやりたくないということだ。
examples.english: In short, he simply doesn't want to do that job.

examples.japanese: 専門的な言葉を使ったが、要するにこの薬は副作用が少ない。
examples.reading: せんもんてきなことばをつかったが、ようするにこのくすりはふくさようがすくない。
examples.english: I used technical terms, but to sum up, this medicine has few side effects.

title: Worst-case contingency 万が一
meaning: Refers to a highly unlikely but possible scenario, used when preparing for or warning about a worst-case event.
explanation: 万が一 literally means 'one in ten thousand' and is used to describe something that has a very slim chance of happening — equivalent to 'just in case,' 'in the unlikely event,' or 'if by any chance' in English. It can function as an adverb or conjunctive and often appears with conditional forms like ～たら or ～の場合. It is neutral in register and common in both spoken warnings and written instructions.

examples.japanese: 万が一、事故が起きた場合には、すぐに連絡してください。
examples.reading: まんがいち、じこがおきたばあいには、すぐにれんらくしてください。
examples.english: In the unlikely event of an accident, please contact us immediately.

examples.japanese: 万が一のために、非常用の食料を備えておくべきだ。
examples.reading: まんがいちのために、ひじょうようのしょくりょうをそなえておくべきだ。
examples.english: You should stock emergency food supplies just in case.

examples.japanese: 万が一、電車が遅れたら、先に始めておいてください。
examples.reading: まんがいち、でんしゃがおくれたら、さきにはじめておいてください。
examples.english: If by any chance the train is late, please go ahead and start without me.

title: Through / throughout / via
meaning: Indicates the means, medium, or duration through which something happens or is accomplished.
explanation: Both を通じて and を通して follow a noun and express (1) the channel or medium through which something is done (via, through), or (2) a period throughout which something continues. を通して tends to emphasize a concrete path or process, while を通じて can also express a continuous state over time and is slightly more formal. Both appear frequently in formal writing and speech.

examples.japanese: 彼はインターネットを通じて世界中の人と繋がっている。
examples.reading: かれはインターネットをつうじてせかいじゅうのひととつながっている。
examples.english: He is connected to people all over the world through the internet.

examples.japanese: この奨学金は、留学を通して得た経験を活かすためのものです。
examples.reading: このしょうがくきんは、りゅうがくをとおしてえたけいけんをいかすためのものです。
examples.english: This scholarship is meant to make use of the experience gained through studying abroad.

examples.japanese: 彼女は一年を通じてほぼ毎日ジョギングをしている。
examples.reading: かのじょはいちねんをつうじてほぼまいにちジョギングをしている。
examples.english: She goes jogging almost every day throughout the year.

title: No choice but / the only option is
meaning: Expresses that there is no alternative; one must do the only thing available.
explanation: よりほかない (also written よりほかはない or よりしかたがない) attaches to the dictionary form of a verb to state that there is literally no other course of action. It is stronger and more resigned in tone than しかない, emphasizing the exhaustion of all alternatives. It can appear in written and spoken formal Japanese and conveys a sense of inevitability or reluctant acceptance.

examples.japanese: 終電を逃してしまったので、歩いて帰るよりほかない。
examples.reading: しゅうでんをのがしてしまったので、あるいてかえるよりほかない。
examples.english: Since I missed the last train, I have no choice but to walk home.

examples.japanese: これ以上の交渉は無駄なので、妥協するよりほかない。
examples.reading: これいじょうのこうしょうはむだなので、だきょうするよりほかない。
examples.english: Since further negotiation is pointless, there is no other option but to compromise.

examples.japanese: 誰も助けてくれないなら、自分でやるよりほかないだろう。
examples.reading: だれもたすけてくれないなら、じぶんでやるよりほかないだろう。
examples.english: If no one is going to help, I suppose there's nothing for it but to do it myself.

title: てはならない
meaning: Must not, Cannot, Should not
explanation: Grammar explanation for てはならない

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

title: Apparently / it seems
meaning: Expresses the speaker's inference that something appears to be the case based on indirect evidence.
explanation: どうやら is an adverb indicating that the speaker has reached a conclusion through observation or indirect cues, not from direct knowledge. It conveys a tentative, inferred judgment and often pairs with らしい, ようだ, or みたいだ at the end of the sentence. It is neutral in register and common in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: どうやら電車が遅れているようだ。
examples.reading: どうやらでんしゃがおくれているようだ。
examples.english: It seems the train is running late.

examples.japanese: どうやら彼女は今日来られないらしい。
examples.reading: どうやらかのじょはきょうこられないらしい。
examples.english: Apparently she won't be able to come today.

examples.japanese: 話を聞いていると、どうやら二人はもう別れたみたいだ。
examples.reading: はなしをきいていると、どうやらふたりはもうわかれたみたいだ。
examples.english: Listening to what people are saying, it appears those two have already broken up.

title: To be careful about / To watch out for
meaning: Expresses deliberately paying attention to something or taking care to avoid a problem.
explanation: に気をつける attaches to nouns to indicate being mindful of or watching out for something. It is commonly used for safety, health, manners, and quality control. It can be used as advice (気をつけてください), a personal intention (気をつけています), or a reminder. It is standard polite Japanese and widely used in both casual and formal contexts.

examples.japanese: 冬は健康に気をつけて、十分な睡眠を取るようにしましょう。
examples.reading: ふゆはけんこうにきをつけて、じゅうぶんなすいみんをとるようにしましょう。
examples.english: In winter, take care of your health and make sure to get enough sleep.

examples.japanese: 夜道を歩く時は、足元に気をつけてください。
examples.reading: よみちをあるくときは、あしもとにきをつけてください。
examples.english: Please watch your step when walking on a dark road at night.

examples.japanese: スピーチでは言葉の使い方に気をつけるようにしている。
examples.reading: スピーチではことばのつかいかたにきをつけるようにしている。
examples.english: When giving speeches, I try to be careful about my choice of words.

title: Regardless of — unconditional inclusion
meaning: Means 'regardless of' or 'without distinction,' indicating that something applies equally to all cases within a category.
explanation: を問わず follows a noun (or paired nouns like 男女, 昼夜) and expresses that a condition, rule, or fact applies without exception across a stated variable. It is formal in tone and appears frequently in written notices, regulations, and announcements. It is interchangeable with にかかわらず in many contexts but sounds slightly more formal.

examples.japanese: このイベントは年齢を問わず、誰でも参加できます。
examples.reading: このイベントはねんれいをとわず、だれでもさんかできます。
examples.english: This event is open to anyone, regardless of age.

examples.japanese: 天候を問わず、試合は予定通りに行われる。
examples.reading: てんこうをとわず、しあいはよていどおりにおこなわれる。
examples.english: The match will be held as scheduled, regardless of weather conditions.

examples.japanese: 経験の有無を問わず、応募することができます。
examples.reading: けいけんのうむをとわず、おうぼすることができます。
examples.english: You can apply whether or not you have prior experience.

title: At last / more and more (culmination adverb)
meaning: Indicates that a long-anticipated event is finally happening, or that a situation is intensifying further.
explanation: いよいよ is an adverb used when something has been building up and is now reaching a decisive or culminating point. It can mean 'finally' (a waited-for moment arrives) or 'more and more / increasingly' (a trend escalating). It carries a sense of heightened anticipation or urgency. It is used in both casual and formal speech, and commonly appears in announcements or narratives.

examples.japanese: いよいよ試験が始まる。
examples.reading: いよいよしけんがはじまる。
examples.english: The exam is finally starting.
